name: Phineas Newborn Jr.
abstract: Phineas Newborn Jr. (December 14, 1931 – May 26, 1989) was an American jazz pianist, whose principal influences were Art Tatum, Oscar Peterson, and Bud Powell.

about_quotes.speaker: Buster Williams
about_quotes.evidence: Horace Silver sounded totally different from Oscar Peterson, who sounded totally different from Art Tatum, who sounded totally different from Fats Waller, who sounded totally different from Phineas Newborn.
about_quotes.doc_title: Buster Williams interviewed by Monk Rowe, New York City, New York, January 6, 2002

about_quotes.speaker: Marian McPartland
about_quotes.evidence: I never felt that I was competing with those people. In my mind was that they were all on a much higher level, that those were people I only thought of in terms of great admiration for what they were doing
about_quotes.doc_title: Marian McPartland — Smithsonian Jazz Oral History

about_quotes.speaker: Terry Gibbs
about_quotes.evidence: Phineas Newborn’s trio. Bud Powell’s trio. Lester Young’s trio. Zoot Sims, Chet Baker’s group and Jeri Southern. That was one show.
about_quotes.doc_title: Terry Gibbs interviewed by Monk Rowe, New York City, New York, January 12, 2001 [interview 1 of 2]

about_quotes.speaker: Mose Allison
about_quotes.evidence: The interviewee does not mention Phineas Newborn; the mention is solely by the interviewer in the context of Memphis.
about_quotes.doc_title: Mose Allison — Smithsonian Jazz Oral History

about_quotes.speaker: Harold Mabern
about_quotes.evidence: I mean everybody knows how I feel about Ahmad and Phineas, that those are my two favorite piano players bar none
about_quotes.doc_title: Harold Mabern interviewed by Monk Rowe, Clinton, New York, March 31, 2010

about_quotes.speaker: Quentin Jackson
about_quotes.evidence: Phineas Newborn, of the young pianists, to me is the greatest, of the young pianists.
